docs.title: 🚀 Fluxion Aggregator Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the official developer documentation for the Fluxion Aggregator Framework — a high-performance rule engine and aggregation system inspired by MongoDB, optimized for complex nested pipelines and ecommerce use cases.

docs.title: 📦 What is Fluxion?
docs.text:
Fluxion is a MongoDB-like aggregation engine built for structured JSON documents. It supports:

  • 40+ Aggregation Stages
  • 200+ Expression Operators
  • System Variables ($$ROOT, $$CURRENT, $$NOW, $$REMOVE, etc.)
  • Deeply Nested Pipelines
  • Advanced support for $facet, $group, $bucket, $map, $filter, $reduce, $switch, $expr, $lookup, and more.

docs.title: 🎯 Why Use Fluxion?
docs.text:
✅ Familiar MongoDB-style syntax ✅ Works on any Python backend ✅ Supports system variables and expressions ✅ Ideal for ecommerce & analytics pipelines ✅ Fully unit-tested and modular

Start exploring from the sidebar or open the Usage Guide to begin!

docs.title: 📦 Key Concepts
docs.text:
Pipeline A series of stages executed in order on a document stream.

Stage A pipeline step (e.g., $match, $group, $project) that transforms documents.

Operator An expression keyword like $sum, $map, $add, used inside stages.

Accumulator Operators like $sum, $avg, $min, $max used within $group.

System Variables - $$ROOT: The entire document. - $$CURRENT: The current level of the document. - $$NOW: Current timestamp. - $$REMOVE: Removes a field from projection. - $$CLUSTER_TIME: Simulated logical timestamp.

Executor The Python engine that executes the pipeline logic.

Expression Any logic within fields: computations, filters, conditions.

docs.title: 📈 Fluxion Roadmap and Version History
docs.text: This document tracks the history of Fluxion's development phases, features delivered in each phase, and potential future enhancements.

docs.title: ✅ Phase 2: Extended Stages
docs.text:
• Added $limit, $skip, $unwind, $set, $unset
  • Field extraction and assignment helpers
  • Basic memory optimization (shallow copy)

docs.title: ✅ Phase 3: Deep Expression Language
docs.text:
• Expression Dispatcher (expression_dispatcher.py) with full operator system
  • Support for $sum, $avg, $min, $max, $add, $subtract, $multiply, $divide
  • Early testing framework set up

docs.title: ✅ Phase 4: Complex Pipelines
docs.text:
• Nested stages support ($facet, $lookup)
  • Foreign collections simulation for $lookup
  • Variable resolution ($$ROOT, $$CURRENT)

docs.title: ✅ Phase 5: Full Aggregation Validation
docs.text:
• $bucket, $bucketAuto, $replaceRoot, $replaceWith
  • Deep pipeline tests on ecommerce dataset
  • Mega test suites: multi-stage, multi-facet pipelines

docs.title: ✅ Phase 7: Merge, Out, Decimal
docs.text:
• Simulated $merge and $out stages
  • Decimal128 (high-precision math) support
  • Error Hardening: Input validations

docs.title: ✅ Phase 8: Memory Optimization and Cursor API
docs.text:
• Optimized executor to avoid unnecessary copying
  • Added execute_cursor() API for lightweight streaming
  • Basic large dataset handling (tested with 1M+ docs)

docs.title: 🔵 Phase 10: Advanced System Enhancements
docs.text: Plan Description Phase 10A: Internal Streaming Execution Deep stage-by-stage document streaming (generator chaining) Phase 10B: Parallel Stage Execution Parallelize independent branches (like $facet) Phase 10C: Query Optimizer Smart pipeline reordering ($match first, $project early) Phase 10D: SQL-to-Aggregation Compiler Accept SQL-like queries, compile to pipelines Phase 10E: External Connectors Attach Fluxion to real MongoDB, PostgreSQL, ElasticSearch

docs.text:
✅ Fluxion is production-ready for in-memory document aggregation.

✅ Matches 90–95% of real MongoDB aggregation use-cases.

✅ Extensible and easily embeddable in custom projects.

docs.title: 📘 Usage Guide
docs.text: This guide walks you through using Fluxion pipelines for common aggregation tasks.

docs.title: ✅ 1. Creating a Pipeline
docs.text:
A pipeline is an array of stages:

[
  { "$match": { "status": "active" } },
  { "$group": { "_id": "$category", "total": { "$sum": "$amount" } } }
]

docs.title: ✅ 2. Running a Pipeline
docs.text:
You can run it via the MongoPipelineExecutor:

from aggregator.executor import MongoPipelineExecutor

executor = MongoPipelineExecutor()
result = executor.execute(documents, pipeline)

docs.title: ✅ 4. Advanced Nesting
docs.text:
You can nest operators like:

{
  "$addFields": {
    "final_score": {
      "$reduce": {
        "input": "$scores",
        "initialValue": 0,
        "in": { "$add": ["$$value", "$$this"] }
      }
    }
  }
}

For more examples, see the Examples Gallery.

docs.title: Connector Module Overview
docs.text: Fluxion Connect packages the source and sink connectors that plug into the streaming runtime.

docs.title: Connector Model
docs.text:
• SourceConnectorProvider describes each connector, exposing option metadata for validation.
  • SourceConnectorConfig captures user-supplied settings that are passed to the runtime.
  • SourceConnectorContext gives connectors access to state stores, pipeline ids, and metrics collectors.
  • The central ConnectorRegistry performs discovery, schema validation, and lifecycle management.

docs.title: Built-in Connectors
docs.text:
• Kafka source provider ships out of the box and demonstrates the full SPI.
  • Additional connectors (HTTP polling, JDBC CDC, filesystem tailers, etc.) can register at runtime via ConnectorFactory.registerSource.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Review the Usage Guide for configuration patterns shared across modules.
  • Explore the Roadmap to track upcoming connector work.
  • When adding a connector, document its option schema under this section so downstream teams can configure it quickly.

docs.title: Kafka Connectors
docs.text: Fluxion exposes Kafka as both a streaming source and sink. The connectors reuse the SPI we use for every transport, so the configuration is intentionally symmetrical: serializers, security settings, batching behaviour, and any additional Kafka property can be injected directly.

docs.title: Source (type: kafka)
docs.text:
Use the source connector to subscribe to a topic and push records into a pipeline. Each record is converted to a Fluxion document (_meta carries Kafka metadata such as topic, partition, offset, timestamp, and key).

source:
  type: kafka
  options:
    topic: orders
    bootstrapServers: localhost:9092
    pollTimeout: PT0.5S        # optional ISO-8601 duration
    queueCapacity: 64          # internal queue between consumer and stages
    keyDeserializer: org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
    valueDeserializer: org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer
    securityProtocol: SASL_SSL
    saslMechanism: PLAIN
    saslJaasConfig: >
      org.apache.kafka.common.security.plain.PlainLoginModule required
      username="user" password="pass";
    enable.auto.commit: false  # additional Kafka option is passed through
Option Description Default topic Kafka topic to subscribe to. Required bootstrapServers Comma-separated broker list. Required pollTimeout Wait time when no records are returned. PT0.5S queueCapacity Size of the internal queue feeding the next stage. 16 keyDeserializer Kafka key deserializer class. StringDeserializer valueDeserializer Kafka value deserializer class. StringDeserializer securityProtocol Optional security.protocol override (e.g. SSL, SASL_SSL). null saslMechanism Optional sasl.mechanism (e.g. SCRAM-SHA-512). null saslJaasConfig JAAS config string when SASL is enabled. null groupId Overrides consumer group id. fluxion-<topic>

All other keys under options are copied straight into the consumer Properties, so you can configure commits, partition assignment, etc.

docs.title: Sink (type: kafka)
docs.text:
The sink batches JSON-encoded documents, tracks success/failure statistics, and flushes once every batch or when the timeout expires. If an exception occurs we bubble it up so the streaming error-policy can react (retry, dead-letter, skip).

sink:
  type: kafka
  options:
    topic: orders-out
    bootstrapServers: localhost:9092
    batchSize: 200
    flushTimeout: PT5S
    acks: all
    keySerializer: org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer
    valueSerializer: org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringSerializer
    securityProtocol: SASL_SSL
    saslMechanism: PLAIN
    saslJaasConfig: >
      org.apache.kafka.common.security.plain.PlainLoginModule required
      username="user" password="pass";
    compression.type: gzip
Option Description Default topic Kafka topic to publish to. Required bootstrapServers Comma-separated broker list. Required batchSize Number of documents per send batch. 100 flushTimeout ISO-8601 duration to wait for all acknowledgements. PT10S acks Producer acknowledgement level (acks). 1 keySerializer Kafka key serializer class. StringSerializer valueSerializer Kafka value serializer class. StringSerializer securityProtocol Optional security.protocol. null saslMechanism Optional sasl.mechanism. null saslJaasConfig JAAS config string when SASL is enabled. null

Additional producer properties (idempotence, linger.ms, compression, etc.) are copied straight onto the Kafka producer configuration.

docs.title: Operational Notes
docs.text:
• Metrics: the sink tracks successful and failed records plus batch counts; wire these into your monitoring layer using the streaming metrics APIs.
  • Retry / error policies: combine the sink with StreamingErrorPolicy to control retries, dead-letter routing, or batch skipping if Kafka becomes unavailable.
  • Testing: the codebase uses Kafka Testcontainers for integration tests. If you need a local broker, run docker run --rm -p 9092:9092 confluentinc/cp-kafka (or reuse the compose file in your project).

docs.title: Core Platform Overview
docs.text: Fluxion Core delivers the aggregation engine, streaming runtime, and shared services that power every module in the platform.

docs.title: Aggregation Engine
docs.text:
• MongoDB-compatible pipeline language with 200+ operators and 40+ stages.
  • Expression evaluation reuses the same engine across batch and streaming flows.
  • System variables ($$ROOT, $$CURRENT, $$NOW, $$REMOVE, etc.) are fully supported.

docs.title: Streaming Runtime
docs.text:
• StreamingPipelineOrchestrator wires sources, stages, and sinks using StreamingPipelineDefinition.
  • StreamingRuntimeConfig exposes knobs for queue capacity, worker pool size, and backpressure.
  • Configurable error policies (StreamingErrorPolicy, skip/dead-letter routing) keep pipelines resilient.
  • Sources implement lifecycle hooks (pause, resume, cancel, checkpoint) through StreamingSource.

docs.title: Observability & Tooling
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry exporters push metrics and traces over OTLP (gRPC); configure OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS, and OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES to point at your collector/back-end.
  • /metrics endpoint exposes Prometheus-format counters/gauges (append ?format=json for JSON) for stage invocations, throughput, and queue depth—ready for a sidecar scrape.
  • /traces endpoint streams recent spans (JSON) captured via the lightweight in-process tracer; each span carries attributes + exceptions for downstream analysis.
  • Connector registry handles discovery and validation of streaming connectors.
  • Extensive JUnit coverage under fluxion-core ensures behaviour stays stable across refactors.

Tip: set OTEL_SERVICE_NAME (defaults to fluxion-core) alongside the OTLP endpoint to tag metrics and spans with your preferred service identity.

Use the navigation to explore:

  • Usage Guide for end-to-end setup.
  • Stages for pipeline building blocks.
  • Operators for expression language details.

docs.title: Enrichment Module Overview
docs.text: Fluxion Enrich extends the aggregation engine with operators that call out to external services or data stores during pipeline evaluation.

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
• Declarative service calls via $httpCall, with support for dynamic headers, params, request bodies, and JSON pointer extraction.
  • SQL lookups through $sqlQuery, binding expression results into prepared statements and mapping rows back to documents.
  • Shared Resilience4j scaffolding (retry + circuit breaker) that mirrors the patterns used in the streaming runtime.
  • Connector registry integration so operators can reference named HTTP or SQL connections managed by the platform.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• $httpCall operator
  • $sqlQuery operator
  • Examples gallery for real-world pipelines that mix core and enrichment features.

Additional enrichment helpers (batch HTTP, cached lookups, etc.) will be documented here as they are implemented.

docs.title: $httpCall
docs.text: Performs an HTTP request during pipeline evaluation. Requests can target a literal URL or reuse a named HttpConnector for shared configuration.

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Field Description Default connection Name of the registered HttpConnector providing base URL, headers, or auth. Optional when url is supplied. null url Request URL template. Path parameters wrapped in {} are substituted with resolved params. Required if connection does not specify a URL. Connector URL method HTTP method (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, etc.). GET headers Map of header names to expressions. Resolved per request. {} params Key/value expressions appended as query string parameters and used for {param} replacements. {} body Expression resolved to build the request body. Objects are JSON encoded automatically. null responsePath JSON Pointer applied to the decoded response body (e.g., /data/items/0). Entire response connectTimeoutMs Connection timeout in milliseconds. 5000 readTimeoutMs Read timeout in milliseconds. 5000 retry Resilience4j retry configuration (see Resilience Patterns). Disabled circuitBreaker Resilience4j circuit breaker configuration (see Resilience Patterns). Disabled

ℹ️ Refer to the shared Resilience Patterns page for the full list of retry and circuit-breaker fields with defaults.

docs.title: Response Handling
docs.text:
• Responses are parsed as JSON. Primitive or array responses are preserved.
  • When responsePath is supplied, the pointer is resolved against the parsed payload.
  • HTTP status codes ≥ 400 cause the operator to throw, participating in retry/breaker logic if enabled.

docs.title: Connectors
docs.text:
HttpConnector instances allow you to centralise base URLs, default headers, and authentication tokens:

ConnectorManager.register("userService", new HttpConnector(
    "https://profiles/api/users",
    Map.of("Authorization", "Bearer ${TOKEN}")
));

Operators reference the connector by name, layering additional headers or overriding the URL as needed.

docs.title: Resilience
docs.text: Supplying a unique name lets multiple pipelines share the same Resilience4j state. See Resilience Patterns for configuration details and best practices.

docs.title: $sqlQuery
docs.text: Executes a parameterised SQL statement against a SqlConnector that has been registered with the enrichment connector registry.

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Field Description Default connection Name of the SqlConnector registered in ConnectorManager. Required query SQL string to execute. Use positional ? parameters for binding. Required params Array of expressions resolved before execution and bound in order. [] expectSingle When true, returns only the first row (or null when no rows). Otherwise returns the entire result set as a list of maps. false name Friendly name used to derive retry/breaker ids when none are supplied. Derived from connection + query hash retry Resilience4j retry configuration (see Resilience Patterns). Disabled circuitBreaker Resilience4j circuit breaker configuration (see Resilience Patterns). Disabled

ℹ️ The shared Resilience Patterns page lists every retry and circuit-breaker field with defaults and guidance.

docs.title: Returned Value
docs.text:
• When expectSingle is true, a single row is returned as a map keyed by column label.
  • Otherwise, the operator returns a list of row maps.
  • If no rows are returned and expectSingle is true, the value is null.

docs.title: Testing Notes
docs.text:
Integration tests under fluxion-enrich use an in-memory H2 database to exercise:

  • Successful single-row and multi-row queries.
  • Retry behaviour on transient connection failures.
  • Circuit breaker transitions for repeated failures.
  • Disabled breaker scenarios to confirm baseline error propagation.

docs.title: $add
docs.text: The $add operator adds numbers together or concatenates dates and numeric expressions.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$add": [ <expression1>, <expression2>, ... ] }

Each expression should resolve to a number or date.

docs.title: 🧱 Deep Nested Example – Compute Total for Each Item in an Order
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Calculating final prices
  • Summing quantities
  • Adding durations to timestamps

docs.title: 🔗 Related
docs.text:
• $subtract, $multiply, $divide – arithmetic family
  • $project, $group – stages where $add is commonly used

docs.title: $allElementsTrue
docs.text: The $allElementsTrue operator returns true if every element in the input array evaluates to a truthy value.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Verify that every value meets a condition
  • Check that all features/options are enabled
  • Chain with $map, $filter

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Returns true for an empty array
  • Each element is evaluated using JavaScript-style truthiness

docs.title: $anyElementTrue
docs.text: The $anyElementTrue operator returns true if any element of the input array evaluates to a truthy value.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Check If Any Truthy
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Check for Available Features
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Detect at least one match
  • Validate presence of flags or statuses
  • Use after $map or $filter

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Returns false for an empty array.
  • Converts each element to boolean using JavaScript-like truthiness.

docs.title: $arrayElemAt
docs.text: The $arrayElemAt operator returns the element at a specified index in an array.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$arrayElemAt": [ <arrayExpression>, <index> ] }
  • Indexing starts at 0.
  • Negative indexes count from the end.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Access top/last items
  • Index-based lookups
  • Extract highlights or featured content

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Index out of bounds returns null.
  • Combine with $size or $cond for safer access.

docs.title: $arrayToObject
docs.text: The $arrayToObject operator transforms an array of key-value documents into a single document (object).

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$arrayToObject": <arrayExpression> }

Each array element must be a document with "k" (key) and "v" (value) fields.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Convert Array to Object
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Reconstruct flattened key-value arrays
  • Transform map-reduce style output
  • Enable object-style access to filtered key-value results

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Input array must be in [{k, v}] format
  • Duplicate keys will overwrite earlier values

docs.title: $concat
docs.text: The $concat operator joins strings together into a single string.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$concat": [ <expression1>, <expression2>, ... ] }

All expressions must resolve to strings. If any operand resolves to null, the result is null.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Formatting names and addresses
  • Constructing SKUs or file paths
  • HTML or CSV generation

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Ensure all values are strings or cast them using $toString.
  • Use $cond or $ifNull to avoid null operands.

docs.title: $concatArrays
docs.text: The $concatArrays operator merges two or more arrays into a single array.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Merge multiple inventories
  • Combine feature lists
  • Append new values to arrays

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Works only on arrays; input expressions must evaluate to arrays.
  • Null or missing inputs are treated as null and return null.

docs.title: $cond
docs.text:
The $cond operator is a conditional (if–then–else) operator.

It evaluates a condition and returns one of two expressions depending on whether the condition is true or false.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Apply different logic based on price, quantity, status
  • Select values conditionally inside $project, $group, $map
  • Use inside $reduce, $switch, $filter

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Flexible control structure in aggregation pipelines
  • Both array and object syntax are supported by MongoDB (and Fluxion)

docs.title: $convert
docs.text: The $convert operator explicitly converts a value to a specified type, optionally handling onNull and onError fallbacks.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$convert": {
    "input": <expression>,
    "to": <type>,
    "onError": <fallback>,     // optional
    "onNull": <fallback>       // optional
  }
}
  • input: The value to convert
  • to: Target type (string, int, double, bool, date, objectId)
  • onError: Value to return if conversion fails
  • onNull: Value to return if input is null or missing

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Convert String to Integer
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Convert Product Launch Date
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Format validation and casting
  • Prevent failures during transformation
  • Normalize mixed-type inputs

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Conversion errors trigger onError, not pipeline failure
  • Use $type to debug values before converting

docs.title: $divide
docs.text: The $divide operator divides one number by another.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$divide": [ <numerator>, <denominator> ] }

Both arguments must resolve to numeric values. Division by zero will result in an error or null.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Convert Cents to Dollars
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Compute Unit Price per Item
📌 Expression
docs.text:
{ "$divide": ["$discount", "$price"] }

Used to compute the proportion of discount relative to the price.

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If denominator is 0, the result may be null or cause an error.
  • Always validate for zero if dynamic input is used.

docs.title: $filter
docs.text: The $filter operator selects elements from an array that satisfy a condition.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$filter": {
    "input": <arrayExpression>,
    "as": <variableName>,
    "cond": <booleanExpression>
  }
}
  • input: The array to filter
  • as: The variable name for each item
  • cond: The condition that must be true to include the item

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Select items matching a filter
  • Clean arrays of null/empty/invalid entries
  • Apply logic to sub-documents or features

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use $$ to access loop variables.
  • Useful for filtering ecommerce features, specs, or product tags.

docs.title: $first
docs.text: The $first operator returns the first element in an array or the first document in a group.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax (Aggregation Accumulator)
docs.text:
{ "$first": <expression> }
  • Used inside $group, $setWindowFields, or in array expressions like $map, $reduce.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Get First Ordered Item
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Get the first record in a group
  • Pick the first element of an array
  • Use in $group, $project, $reduce

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• In $group, $first takes the value from the first document in the group — order matters!
  • In arrays, it behaves like arr[0].

docs.title: $function
docs.text: The $function operator lets you define and execute custom logic using JavaScript-like syntax within a pipeline.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$function": {
    "body": <functionCodeString>,
    "args": [ <arg1>, <arg2>, ... ],
    "lang": "js"
  }
}
  • body: JavaScript function code as a string
  • args: Array of arguments passed to the function
  • lang: Must be "js" (Fluxion-safe context)

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Advanced conditional logic
  • Complex business formulas
  • Processing values beyond built-in operators

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Fluxion executes $function securely and deterministically.
  • Avoid side effects; treat as pure functions.

docs.title: $getField
docs.text: The $getField operator retrieves the value of a specified field from a document, supporting both static and dynamic keys.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$getField": {
    "field": <fieldName>,
    "input": <document>
  }
}
  • field: Name of the field to access (string or expression)
  • input: The document to read from (optional — defaults to current document)

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Access Variant Detail by Dynamic Key
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Dynamically access fields
  • Fetch variable-key metadata
  • Pair with $map or $objectToArray for dynamic access

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Avoids $[dot] notation for dynamic fields
  • Returns null if the field does not exist

docs.title: $ifNull
docs.text: The $ifNull operator returns the first non-null expression from the provided list of two expressions.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Provide fallback/default values
  • Avoid nulls in views or exports
  • Chain with $cond or $switch

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Returns expression1 if it's not null or missing
  • Otherwise returns fallback

docs.title: $indexOfArray
docs.text: The $indexOfArray operator returns the index of the first occurrence of a value in an array.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$indexOfArray": [ <arrayExpression>, <searchValue>, <start>, <end> ] }
  • arrayExpression: The array to search
  • searchValue: The value to search for
  • start: (optional) Index to start searching from
  • end: (optional) Index to end searching (exclusive)

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Find Index of Value
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Position of Top-Selling Product
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Determine item positions
  • Ranking systems
  • Validation of list order

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Returns -1 if not found.
  • Use with $cond to handle missing values gracefully.

docs.title: $indexOfBytes
docs.text: The $indexOfBytes operator returns the byte index of the first occurrence of a substring in a string.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$indexOfBytes": [ <string>, <search>, <start>, <end> ] }
  • string: The string to search
  • search: Substring to find
  • start (optional): Start index (inclusive)
  • end (optional): End index (exclusive)

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Check for Brand Code in SKU
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Find substring location
  • Validate presence or order of fields
  • Index-based manipulation

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Operates on byte positions (not Unicode-safe).
  • Use $indexOfCP for multi-byte character safety.

docs.title: $indexOfCP
docs.text: The $indexOfCP operator returns the character (code point) index of the first occurrence of a substring in a string.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$indexOfCP": [ <string>, <search>, <start>, <end> ] }
  • string: The string to search
  • search: The substring to find
  • start (optional): Index to start searching (default 0)
  • end (optional): Index to stop searching (exclusive)

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Check for Prefix in SKU Labels
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Locate substrings safely in multilingual data
  • Index slicing or truncation
  • Filtering records with presence check

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use $indexOfCP when string may include multibyte characters (e.g. emojis, Hindi, Chinese).
  • Returns -1 if not found.

docs.title: $isArray
docs.text: The $isArray operator checks whether a given expression resolves to an array.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$isArray": <expression> }

Returns true if the result is an array, otherwise false.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Input validation
  • Conditional logic on array-dependent fields
  • Filter dynamic JSON structures

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use in combination with $cond to branch logic.
  • Helps clean malformed documents with inconsistent schemas.

docs.title: $last
docs.text: The $last operator returns the last element in an array or the last document in a group.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Last Item in Cart by Time
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Return last item in array
  • Use with sorted groups
  • Useful in $group, $reduce, or windowed analysis

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• In $group, the result depends on the order of documents.
  • On arrays, behaves like accessing the last index.

docs.title: $literal
docs.text: The $literal operator returns a constant value without interpreting it as a field path or expression.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$literal": <value> }
  • <value> can be a string, number, object, array, or other raw constant.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Return a Constant String
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Inject Static Tax Field
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Force a static value in dynamic documents
  • Prevent evaluation of field paths or expressions
  • Embed objects or arrays literally

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Useful inside $project, $group, $addFields
  • Does not evaluate inner values — used exactly as written

docs.title: $ltrim
docs.text: The $ltrim operator removes specified characters from the beginning (left) of a string.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Trim Leading Zeros from ID
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Trim leading symbols, padding characters
  • Normalize document IDs or SKUs
  • Clean up data during import

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If chars is not specified, whitespace is removed.
  • For both sides, use $trim.

docs.title: $map
docs.text: The $map operator applies a transformation expression to each element of an input array.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$map": {
    "input": <arrayExpression>,
    "as": <variableName>,
    "in": <expression>
  }
}
  • input: The array to iterate
  • as: The name of the variable for each element
  • in: The expression to evaluate for each element

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Compute Total per Item
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Transform items in an array
  • Compute fields per subdocument
  • Prepare output for $project or $group

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use $$ prefix when referencing variables in $map.
  • Input must be an array; otherwise, returns null.

docs.title: $max
docs.text: The $max operator returns the highest value from a list of expressions or grouped values.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Maximum Product Price Per Brand
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Determine highest price, score, value
  • Identify latest timestamps
  • Combine with $group for summary statistics

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Can be used directly in array or accumulator contexts
  • Behavior on null values depends on placement/order

docs.title: $mergeObjects
docs.text: The $mergeObjects operator merges multiple documents (objects) into a single document. Later fields overwrite earlier ones.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$mergeObjects": [ <doc1>, <doc2>, ... ] }
  • Accepts an array of documents or document expressions
  • Fields in later documents override those in earlier ones

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Merge attribute sets
  • Combine multiple partial documents
  • Override fields selectively

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If merging non-documents (e.g., null, arrays), behavior may vary
  • Null values are ignored unless merged explicitly

docs.title: $min
docs.text: The $min operator returns the smallest value from a list of expressions or grouped values.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Minimum Discounted Price Per Brand
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Calculate lowest value in a group
  • Use in price comparisons
  • Extract min date/time in event logs

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• In $group, must be used with grouped documents
  • Use with caution on mixed-type arrays

docs.title: $mod
docs.text: The $mod operator returns the remainder of the division of one number by another.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$mod": [ <dividend>, <divisor> ] }

Both values must resolve to numbers. Division by zero returns an error.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 1 – Modulo with Even/Odd Check
✅ Base Example 2 – Minutes Past the Hour
📤 Output
docs.text:
[
  { "name": "Item A", "isEligibleForDiscount": true },
  { "name": "Item B", "isEligibleForDiscount": false },
  { "name": "Item C", "isEligibleForDiscount": false },
  { "name": "Item D", "isEligibleForDiscount": true }
]

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Dividing by zero will return an error.
  • Common in coupon, rule, and scheduling systems.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$multiply": [ <expression1>, <expression2>, ... ] }

Each expression must resolve to a numeric value.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Multiply Price by Quantity
🧱 Deep Nested Example – Compute Line Totals in Order
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Calculating total cost (price * qty)
  • Tax/value scaling
  • Aggregated computations in $group

docs.title: $objectToArray
docs.text: The $objectToArray operator converts a document (object) into an array of key-value pair documents.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$objectToArray": <documentExpression> }

Each entry in the output array has the form:

{ "k": <key>, "v": <value> }

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Convert Object to Array
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Transform object keys into documents
  • Use with $map and $filter on object properties
  • Enable custom aggregation logic for dynamic keys

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Input must be an object; returns error on arrays or null
  • Often paired with $map or $reduce for key-based logic

docs.title: $reduce
docs.text: The $reduce operator applies an expression to each element in an array and accumulates the result.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$reduce": {
    "input": <arrayExpression>,
    "initialValue": <expression>,
    "in": <accumulatorExpression>
  }
}
  • input: The array to iterate
  • initialValue: The starting value of the accumulator
  • in: Expression that updates the accumulator using $$value and $$this

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Total Price of All Items
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Compute totals or nested accumulations
  • String aggregation
  • Custom transformations

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• $$value refers to the accumulator
  • $$this refers to the current array element

docs.title: $regexMatch
docs.text: The $regexMatch operator evaluates whether a string input matches a regular expression pattern.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$regexMatch": {
    "input": <string>,
    "regex": <pattern>,
    "options": <string>
  }
}
  • input: The string to test.
  • regex: The pattern to match.
  • options: Optional flags like "i" (case-insensitive), "m" (multiline), etc.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Validate Product ID Format
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Check Brand Label Format
🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use \ to escape characters like \d in JSON.
  • Combine with $cond or $match for rule evaluation.

docs.title: $reverseArray
docs.text: The $reverseArray operator returns a new array with the elements in reverse order.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Show Most Recent Orders First
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Display latest entries first
  • Change processing order
  • Use before slicing or accessing via index

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Input must be an array; otherwise returns null.
  • Does not modify the original array.

docs.title: $rtrim
docs.text: The $rtrim operator removes specified characters from the end (right) of a string.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$rtrim": {
    "input": <expression>,
    "chars": <charsToTrim>
  }
}
  • input: The string to trim from the right
  • chars: Characters to remove (defaults to whitespace)

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Trim Dashes from End
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Trim Codes for Export
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Remove unwanted trailing symbols
  • Clean up formatting before exporting
  • Strip padding or delimiters from strings

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If chars is omitted, whitespace is removed.
  • Use with $ltrim or $trim to handle full sides.

docs.title: $setField
docs.text: The $setField operator allows adding, updating, or replacing a field in a document.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$setField": {
    "field": <fieldName>,
    "input": <document>,
    "value": <newValue>
  }
}
  • field: The field to set
  • input: The input document
  • value: The new value to assign

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Add Rating to Product Attribute
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Modify nested subdocuments
  • Add calculated fields
  • Create documents with dynamic key-value pairs

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Allows modifying deeply nested fields with full control
  • Supports dynamic field names using expressions

docs.title: $size
docs.text: The $size operator returns the number of elements in an array.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$size": <arrayExpression> }

The input must resolve to an array.

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Count number of tags, features, options
  • Validate minimum/maximum items
  • Use with $cond or $switch for conditional logic

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Returns an error if input is not an array
  • Use $ifNull or $cond for safety

docs.title: $slice
docs.text: The $slice operator returns a subset of an array. You can specify the number of elements to return, and optionally, the starting position.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$slice": [ <arrayExpression>, <n> ] }

or

{ "$slice": [ <arrayExpression>, <start>, <n> ] }
  • arrayExpression: The array to slice.
  • n: The number of elements to return.
  • start: (optional) The index to start slicing from.

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Negative n removes elements from the end.
  • Use with $sort to return top records before slicing.

docs.title: $split
docs.text: The $split operator divides a string into an array of substrings based on a specified delimiter.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Parse Composite SKU Codes
🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If the delimiter is not found, the entire string is returned in a single-element array.
  • Returns an empty array when splitting an empty string.

docs.title: $strLenBytes
docs.text: The $strLenBytes operator returns the length of a string in bytes (not characters).

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$strLenBytes": <expression> }

The expression must resolve to a string.

docs.title: 📤 Output
docs.text:
4

Emoji and many Unicode characters use multiple bytes.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Calculate Byte Size of Labels
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Prepare size-limited exports
  • Analyze string size for storage
  • Truncate safely with $substrBytes

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Byte length ≠ character count in UTF-8.
  • Use $strLenCP for actual character count.

docs.title: $strLenCP
docs.text: The $strLenCP operator returns the length of a string in UTF-8 code points (characters).

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$strLenCP": <expression> }

The expression must resolve to a string.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Validate Product Name Length
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Character validation (min/max lengths)
  • UI truncation
  • Enforcing content size rules

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use $strLenCP when Unicode characters are expected.
  • For byte size, use $strLenBytes.

docs.title: $strcasecmp
docs.text:
The $strcasecmp operator performs a case-insensitive comparison of two strings and returns:

  • 0 if they are equal (ignoring case)
  • 1 if the first is greater
  • -1 if the second is greater

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Compare Category Codes for Sorting
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Case-insensitive comparisons
  • Field normalization for filtering
  • Use in $project, $match, or conditional logic

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Both arguments must be strings.
  • Returns numeric value based on case-insensitive lexicographical comparison.

docs.title: $substr
docs.text: The $substr operator returns a substring of a string, starting at a specified index and with a specified length.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$substr": [ <string>, <start>, <length> ] }
  • <string>: The source string expression
  • <start>: Zero-based start index
  • <length>: Number of characters to return

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Shorten Brand Prefix from SKU
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Extract prefixes, suffixes, or sections
  • Parse embedded strings
  • Simplify field values for display

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• $substr is deprecated in favor of $substrBytes and $substrCP for Unicode correctness.
  • Start index out of range returns an empty string.

docs.title: $substrBytes
docs.text: The $substrBytes operator returns a substring from a string, measured in bytes, starting at a specified index with a specified byte length.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$substrBytes": [ <string>, <startByte>, <byteLength> ] }
  • <string>: The input string
  • <startByte>: Byte offset (starting from 0)
  • <byteLength>: Number of bytes to return

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 1 – Extract First 3 Bytes (ASCII safe)
✅ Base Example 2 – Byte Truncation of File Name
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Shorten Product Codes for Export
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Export byte-limited strings
  • Handling legacy encodings (e.g., ASCII)
  • Efficient slicing in fixed-byte-width systems

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Works best with ASCII or single-byte encodings.
  • May truncate multi-byte Unicode characters incorrectly — for Unicode-safe use $substrCP.

docs.title: $substrCP
docs.text: The $substrCP operator returns a substring from a string, measured in UTF-8 code points (characters), starting at a specified character index with a specified number of characters.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$substrCP": [ <string>, <startChar>, <charLength> ] }
  • <string>: The input string
  • <startChar>: Character index to start from (0-based)
  • <charLength>: Number of characters (code points) to return

docs.title: 🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Multilingual-safe substring extraction
  • Character-length-limited output
  • Text formatting for display

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Use $substrCP over $substr or $substrBytes for Unicode strings.
  • Avoid cutting emojis or multibyte characters with $substrBytes.

docs.title: $subtract

docs.title: 📌 Syntax

{ "$subtract": [ <expression1>, <expression2> ] }

<expression1> is the value to subtract from. <expression2> is the value to subtract.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Subtract Discount from Price
🧱 Deep Nested Example – Compute Remaining Balance per Item

docs.title: $switch
docs.text: The $switch operator allows multiple conditional branches, similar to a traditional switch-case statement.

docs.title: $toLower
docs.text: The $toLower operator converts a string to all lowercase letters.

{ "$toLower": <expression> }

The expression must resolve to a string.

docs.title: $toUpper
docs.text: The $toUpper operator converts a string to all uppercase letters.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{ "$toUpper": <expression> }

The expression must resolve to a string.

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Uppercase Username for Display
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Case normalization
  • Visual formatting for UI
  • Standardizing categories and labels

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Input must be a string.

docs.title: $trim
docs.text: The $trim operator removes specified characters from the beginning and end of a string.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$trim": {
    "input": <expression>,
    "chars": <charsToTrim>
  }
}
  • input: The string to trim
  • chars: The characters to remove (optional, defaults to whitespace)

docs.title: ✅ Base Example 2 – Trim Slashes from Path
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Strip extra characters (slashes, dashes, spaces)
  • Normalize labels and IDs
  • Clean user input

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• If chars is omitted, whitespace is trimmed by default.
  • Useful before comparisons and filtering.

docs.title: $type
docs.text: The $type operator returns a string that identifies the BSON type of the input value.

docs.title: 🧱 Ecommerce Example – Validate Price Type Before Conversion
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Debug complex fields before transformation
  • Conditional conversion or validation
  • Auditing unknown schemas

docs.title: 🧠 Notes
docs.text:
• Useful when working with inconsistent or dynamic data sources
  • Helps avoid runtime conversion errors

docs.title: $unsetField
docs.text: The $unsetField operator removes a field from a document dynamically.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$unsetField": {
    "field": <fieldName>,
    "input": <document>
  }
}

docs.title: $zip
docs.text: The $zip operator merges multiple arrays element-wise into a single array of arrays.

docs.title: 📌 Syntax
docs.text:
{
  "$zip": {
    "inputs": [ <array1>, <array2>, ... ],
    "useLongestLength": <bool>,  // optional
    "defaults": [ <default1>, <default2>, ... ] // optional
  }
}
  • inputs: The arrays to zip together
  • useLongestLength: If true, extends shorter arrays with null or defaults
  • defaults: Values to use when an array runs out of elements

docs.title: ✅ Base Example – Merge Name and Age Lists
✅ With Unequal Length Arrays and Defaults
🧱 Ecommerce Example – Merge Features and Availability
🔧 Common Use Cases
docs.text:
• Merge parallel arrays for display
  • Create structured tabular pairs
